summary: The post argues that deep learning's requirement for massive labeled datasets is fundamentally inferior to how humans learn, who can acquire concepts from as few as one example. Khailo proposes combining deep feature extraction, capsule networks, and Peter Gärdenfors' Conceptual Spaces theory to create 'Deep Latent Space Maps' — a system that partitions learned latent spaces using Voronoi diagrams and voting mechanisms to enable few-shot learning.
key_points: Children learn ~60,000 words by age 18, often from a single example, while neural networks require thousands to millions of examples
Deep feature extraction can map data to latent spaces unsupervised, separating representation learning from label learning
Combining Conceptual Spaces theory with capsule networks and Voronoi partitioning could enable one-shot learning
Grid cell research from neuroscience validates the idea of abstract spatial representations for concepts
money_quotes: I think it's clear to most researchers that Deep Learning has a deep problem because these systems learn in clearly inferior ways.
As I watch my children learn, I am deeply unsatisfied with current techniques and know there must be a better way.
In other words, you can train it using only as little as one example of an object, word or thing.
The interesting bit here is to explore using Peter Gärdenfors idea of how learning works in our brain which new research is increasingly validating. It just feels right.
key_insight: By combining deep feature extraction with Voronoi space partitioning and capsule network voting mechanisms, it may be possible to build learning systems that, like children, can learn new concepts from just one example.
